comment_text.sn11.6:2.1: This verse is also in Ja 31. This delightful story shows the complete transformation of Indra from the war god who revels in slaughter (Atharva Veda 8.8). Indra showed a comparable development in Hinduism, too, for he is said to have saved a wise parrot in his nest when the tree was poisoned (Mahābhārata, Anuśāsanaparva 5).

root_text.sn11.6:1.3: Tasmiṁ kho pana, bhikkhave, saṅgāme asurā jiniṁsu, devā parājiniṁsu.
root_text.sn11.6:1.4: Parājitā ca kho, bhikkhave, devā apāyaṁsveva uttarenamukhā, abhiyaṁsveva ne asurā.
root_text.sn11.6:1.5: Atha kho, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo mātali saṅgāhakaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn11.6:3.1: ‘Evaṁ, bhaddantavā’ti kho, bhikkhave, mātali saṅgāhako sakkassa devānamindassa paṭissutvā sahassayuttaṁ ājaññarathaṁ paccudāvattesi.
root_text.sn11.6:3.3: ‘paccudāvatto kho dāni sakkassa devānamindassa sahassayutto ājaññaratho.
root_text.sn11.6:3.4: Dutiyampi kho devā asurehi saṅgāmessantī’ti bhītā asurapurameva pāvisiṁsu.
root_text.sn11.6:3.5: Iti kho, bhikkhave, sakkassa devānamindassa dhammena jayo ahosī”ti.

translation_text.sn11.6:1.2: “Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans.
translation_text.sn11.6:1.3: In that battle the titans won and the gods lost.
translation_text.sn11.6:1.4: Defeated, the gods fled north with the titans in pursuit.
translation_text.sn11.6:1.5: Then Sakka, lord of gods, addressed his chariot handler Mātali in verse:
translation_text.sn11.6:2.2: in the red silk-cotton woods with your chariot pole.
translation_text.sn11.6:2.3: I’d rather give up our lives to the titans
translation_text.sn11.6:2.4: than deprive these birds of their nests.’
translation_text.sn11.6:3.1: ‘Yes, lord,’ replied Mātali. And he turned the chariot back around, with its team of a thousand thoroughbreds.
translation_text.sn11.6:3.4: The titans will have to fight the gods a second time!’ Terrified, they retreated right away to the citadel of the titans.
translation_text.sn11.6:3.5: And that’s how Sakka came to win victory by principle.”
